speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number eight. Survivor is Benjamin Jacobs. The date is July 16, 1996. What happened to you after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in the hospital, as I stated. And I didn't feel sick. I was-- I urged to go out and see the world, walk like a normal human being without being arrested, without being persecuted. I wanted to see what this feeling is, being a free man. I walked out. And I-- in the number of survivors that I came across in the marketplace of Neustadt, I heard that someone saw my brother. And I found my brother. He was picked up at the last 60 or 70 that hang on tooth and nail to the railing the following morning on May 4th, 24 hours after the ship was bombed, and were brought to shore by the British. We decided to [SIGHS] leave as fast as we could. There were a few friends of mine with me. We went in to a-- there were automobiles everywhere. You could take an automobile for-- if you just could start it, you could take it and go, and if it had gasoline in the tank. And we went in and we took a couple of Fiats, Italian Fiats. Same two cars with war paint. And we drove. We left Auschwitz-- we left Neustadt. We drove south, and we drove into the last place of our internment, the home of Max Schmidt and his parents. And we were greeted by his parents, who said they don't know where Max is, but they will make a good dinner for us and we can stay there overnight. And we had-- they slaughtered the swine, a pig, and made an excellent dinner. And there was alcohol. There was vodka and there was schnapps. And we did not know any end to eating and drinking. And we were all drunk. And when we were drunk, when we were tipsy, when we didn't know what's going on, Max Schmidt showed up with a number tattooed on his arm. He says, you see, I'm one of you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And strangely enough, he had a short talk to us. Sure, he made a short talk to us. He did a short talk saying that things are past. Let's look at the future. We are all alike. And suddenly he was our friend. He hugged me. He hugged everyone that came in. He knew everyone's name. Suddenly he was one of us. And strangely enough, we forgave him. We forgave him because we weren't ourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I woke up the following morning, I realized what happened last night. And I said to my brother and a friend of mine by the name of Izak Lipszyc, who lives now in Detroit, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we have done. How can we sit with this man at the same table? How can we share a meal with his family? How can we do that? What is happening? What are we doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided to leave. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We left, and we stopped at the British depot. And we asked-- we told the British who lives there, that he was our commandant. They were so ignorant. They were so uninterested. They say, we have, we have nothing to do with it. You have to go to the CIC, to the, to the secret service. They weren't interested in arresting him. He is free to this day. In any event, we drove into the American zone. And we landed in Ludenscheid, a small town Westphalia, where one of our friends had an acquaintance there from the [GERMAN], which is the union, workman's union. And they were very kind to us. They were-- the town was very nice to us. They gave us clothing. They gave us housing. They gave us an opportunity to establish a living for ourself. And I couldn't stay in Ludenscheid. I wanted to see the world. And I traveled with this particular gentleman whose name is Herman Joseph. I brought a description about his tactics and his controversial-- and about this controversial person that's in my book. He took me to his home, to his wife, to his children, to his-- and it was outside Nuremberg. But in time, he became involved with the [GERMAN], with the labor unions. And I didn't think that this was my place in society. This was not my cup of tea. I was politically indifferent to German affairs. And I didn't want to live in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did receive from the town major of Nuremberg a car and drove. Wanted to go to France in order to get to the United States. That was always my, my goal to come to the States, the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the road I picked up two boys that were blundering around in the countryside, didn't know where to go. One was from Hungary and one was from Romania. And I took them with me because I saw that they had no destination. They didn't know what to do. And when we went to France, we were not-- they were not very hospitable to us. They put us together with the soldiers overnight. And the next day, they told us we have to go back and apply to French consulate in ordinary terms to normally come to France. That wasn't our destination, so we never pursued that. When I came back, we came to Frankfurt, where a lot of DPs were assembled there. And I became active in organizing food for the DPs because I had a car. And I drove to the airport where I picked up kettles of soup and bread and other things from the American army, and brought it to the hotel that we were staying, the bombed out hotel, until such time that they ordered us to go into another camp that is a DP camp, displaced person camp. No longer a concentration camp, but a camp nevertheless. And I just couldn't go into another camp. I have seen too many camps. So I left, and I returned to Ludenscheid, where I applied to the American consulate to come to the United States, and where I met my wife, to whom I have been married now for a wonderful 46 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Else. Her maiden name was Teichmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married on, on June 11, 1950. I met her in a makeshift temple where her father in-- that we did not have a rabbi, her father was conducting the service, invited me for kiddish. And that was the beginning of a wonderful, wonderful two parents that I had. Mrs. Teichmann and Mr. Teichmann were my adopted parents. I was their adopted son. And I was grateful for giving me their daughter as my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to America on August 22, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what career--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you settle in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I settled in Boston. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to my great-uncle, who has sent an affidavit for my brother and me. And we have lived in Boston since my brother died, unfortunately, in 1965. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hope I still have a few years to fulfill my obligation to those that could not be endowed with the luck and the fortunes that I had to survive. It is something that is very, very important to me. And as I see, it is important to those that I share my fate with. They too are moved. And I can see that my work is making some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's producing some benefit. And I hope that in adding my voice to that of others, in persuading the world that what happened really happened, and what we are are real truths, may submerge the voices of hate against any people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What occupation did you pursue in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a businessman. I could not enter school because the GIs had the preference on the GI Bill of Rights. And my waiting period that-- to enter a dental school was six years, which I couldn't afford. And I have been successful. I have done my part to establish a home for my wife and myself and good [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] means and comfortable life. And I hope that God is willing to give me a few more years to pursue my work that I feel is very important to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any advice you would like to give to the world after all that you endured? What advice would you like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose there is always a final comment to be made. And my final comment is this, that when I ever-- when I-- ever I get to do what I have done with you and with others that I had been interviewed and informally spoken to, high school and universities and colleges, and informally speaking at temples and any assembly of people that want to hear me, that I am speaking to the voice of those that have no voice and cannot tell their story. And they are, in a sense, happy that there are people who can remember them before the world. This is my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say or that you feel you've left out before we end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say that I extend my appreciation and my thanks for the program that Steven Spielberg began and for what you and all those that are with you are doing as volunteers. I think it is incumbent upon us to talk when you would like to have a record of our memories and our survival. Thank you, Louise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your interview and your testimony. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you introduce your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Louise. This is the gal that I spoke of when I recounted the time that I met her in, in, in Germany, thanks to her father inviting me for a kiddish. Here at home I have shared a wonderful 46 years, and hope to continue on to share our life as long as God is willing to keep us on Earth. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just-- I was very lucky to meet my husband when I did. And out of all the misery and everything came a very happy and great relationship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Photograph of my family, part of my family. The upper left, my brother Josek. Next to him to the right is my sister Pola. And the one with the curly hair is I. And Uncle Shlomo is the one on the right. Uncle Shlomo lived in Berlin, Germany. When he visited us in Poland, this picture was taken circa 1935. This photograph is of my father. His name is Awigdor Jakubowicz. The photograph was taken in Poland approximately in 1934 or '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a 1935 photo taken in Dobra, Poland. Far left with the umbrella in hand is I. Sitting to the right is my cousin [? Jadzia. ?] And my sister Pola is next to her, barely visible. Her friend [? Victor ?] [NON-ENGLISH] sits with his face towards the rear, and followed by my mother and my father. This is the only photograph I have of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Ester Jakubowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did say that, 1935. This photograph is of my uncle, Shlomo, and my maternal grandfather, taken in 1936 in Dobra, Poland at one of his annual visits to Dobra from Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name is Hersh-- was Hersh Yankev Jakubowicz. Although he was not related to my father's family, he bore the same name.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's the gentleman on the right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather is on the left or right as you face the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right. This is a photograph of my sister Pola and taken in Dobra, Poland probably in 1936 or '37. Photo of my uncle Izhak, taken in Dobra approximately 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Izhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jakubowicz. The extreme right is my cousin Josek who was in the yellow cavalry in Poland. His last name was also Jakubowicz. Taken in 1930, perhaps, to '31.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dobra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left on this photograph is I. [BACKGROUND NOISE] On the right is my brother Josek. This photograph was-- is a post-war photograph taken in 1946 in Germany, in Ludenscheid, Germany. This photograph was taken in 1985 in July when I was visiting my hometown Dobra. And this is a street sign, or a road sign, that leads to Dobra into the nearby cities Turek and Sieradz. The photograph on the left is I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was also taken in 1985 at front of our house in Dobra. And the door is I. This photograph is also taken at the same time in 1985 of our house. In front of it is, on far left, is the present owner of the house, who acquired it from the Polish government. Next to him is I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the man's name on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't remember. This photograph is of the ship the in her glory days when she was traveling between Hamburg and South America as a luxury ship, carrying some of the upscale people from both countries. This is the ship that we were on when it was sunk on May 3, 1945. And on this and two other ships, the and the total lives lost 15,000 inmates of concentration camps-- Auschwitz, Gross-Rosen, Rosen, and the nearby Hamburg labor camps. This is one of the Juden stars that we had to wear in Warthegau between 1939 September and 1941 May, when I was deported to a labor camp. It is not my star. It is, however, identical to the one that I wore in front and the rear of my clothing in the area of the heart. This photograph is of my memoir, published in 1995 by the University Press of Kentucky. This photograph is of my maternal grandfather Hersh Yankev Jakubowicz. It taken in Dobra probably in 1936, 35. This, this photograph is out of my book,

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the one down below?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph below is, on the far left, my father, Awigdor, my mother, Ester, my sister kneeling, Pola, my brother, Josek, and my cousin Dora. This photograph was taken in 1935 in Dobra, Poland.
